original_abstract: From 1951 to 1991, approximately 17 distinct processes were developed to recover uranium from wet phosphoric acid (WPA); however, the industrial viability of these methods has historically fluctuated alongside volatile market prices. Today, the extraction of uranium from WPA has regained significant interest, driven by rising global demand and the rapid expansion of nuclear energy programs in developing nations. This renewed attention serves as a catalyst for both refining legacy technologies and exploring entirely new methodological approaches. This paper provides a comprehensive review of historical extraction processes and the underlying physicochemistry that governs them. Furthermore, we examine and discuss recent advancements in the development of novel extraction systems aimed at improving efficiency and sustainability in uranium recovery.
humanized_abstract: Between 1951 and 1991 seventeen separate uranium processes were developed from wet phosphoric acid (WPA) uranium recovery technology. The applicability and economic viability of most of these processes have varied with uranium prices. The interest in uranium recovery from wet phosphoric acid is increasing due to the world uranium demand, and the rapid growth of nuclear energy program in developing countries. This has stimulated the optimisation of previous processes as well as the research of new extraction methods. The present paper reviews the various historical uranium processes and their underlying physico-chemistry as well as the more recent developments in new extraction processes.

The interest in uranium recovery from wet phosphoric acid is increasing due to the world uranium demand, and the rapid growth of nuclear energy program in developing countries. This has stimulated the optimisation of previous processes as well as the research of new extraction methods. The present paper reviews the various historical uranium processes and their underlying physico-chemistry as well as the more recent developments in new extraction processes.
